ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bibimbap entry (FOODON_00002572); no specific BBQ beef / bulgogi bibimbap variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJX covers bibimbap as a category; no bulgogi-specific sub-code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for bibimbap or Korean mixed rice dish.

main_ingredients: assorted vegetables (spinach, bean sprouts, carrots, zucchini, mushrooms)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of BBQ Beef Bibimbap is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed rice topped with bulgogi, assorted seasoned vegetables, gochujang sauce, and sesame oil.

nutrient_source.title: Bibimbap with beef — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients and Korean food composition data (NCC / USDA SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and National Nutrient Database for Standard Reference
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for BBQ Beef Bibimbap as a composite dish. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data: cooked white rice (~58% by weight), bulgogi beef (~25%), mixed seasoned vegetables (~12%), sesame oil and gochujang (~5%). Weighted average per 100 g calculated. Cross-referenced with NCC Food and Nutrient Database generic bibimbap category anchor and published Korean dietary studies.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed rice dishes — analogous estimate based on white rice bibimbap; GI of cooked white rice with mixed vegetables and protein components
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for BBQ Beef Bibimbap. GI estimated at 54 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed rice dishes with protein and vegetable components. Plain cooked white rice has GI ~72; the addition of bulgogi beef (protein and fat), seasoned vegetables, and gochujang sauce substantially lowers the effective GI of the composite dish. Analogous mixed rice bowl with meat and vegetables from the University of Sydney database supports GI in the 50–58 range. GL per serving = (54 × 83.25 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 44.9. Available carbohydrate per serving: 18.5 g/100 g × 450 g = 83.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce marinade and gochujang
hypertension.guidance: Each 450 g serving provides approximately 1395 mg sodium, representing 58–70% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2400 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice base, partially offset by protein and fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 54 and a glycemic load of approximately 44.9 per standard serving, BBQ Beef Bibimbap poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion (250–300 g), substitute brown rice if available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and sodium from marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving contributes approximately 5.9 g saturated fat and 1395 mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean cuts of beef, request lower-sodium soy sauce, and limit frequency of consumption to align with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: BBQ Beef Bibimbap (불고기 비빔밥; 烤牛肉拌饭) is a Korean mixed rice bowl dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with bulgogi — thinly sliced beef marinated in a blend of soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and sugar, then grilled or stir-fried — alongside an assortment of seasoned vegetables such as blanched spinach, sautéed bean sprouts, julienned carrots, zucchini, and shiitake mushrooms. The dish is finished with a dollop of gochujang (Korean fermented chilli paste) and a drizzle of sesame oil, and is traditionally mixed thoroughly before eating, giving the dish its name: 'bibim' (mixed) and 'bap' (rice). A fried or raw egg yolk is often added. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly sweet from the bulgogi marinade, mildly spicy from gochujang, and nutty from sesame oil. Texturally, the dish combines soft rice, tender beef, and varied vegetable textures. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g serving provides approximately 666 kcal, 36.9 g protein, 18.5 g fat, and 83.3 g carbohydrate, making it a balanced meal with moderate glycemic impact (estimated GI 54). The sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce and gochujang, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a staple of Korean cuisine and is widely available in Korean restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in BBQ beef bibimbap?
Is bulgogi bibimbap good for diabetics?
Can I eat bibimbap if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of bibimbap?
How much protein is in bulgogi bibimbap?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for BBQ Beef Bibimbap as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked white rice, bulgogi beef, and seasoned vegetables, weighted by approximate proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable mixed rice dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component data for this composite dish. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated values from component analysis. Sodium content is notably elevated due to soy sauce in the bulgogi marinade and gochujang; restaurant preparations may vary significantly. The halal status is listed as false because standard bulgogi marinades use soy sauce which may contain alcohol-based additives, and beef sourcing is not confirmed halal; individual restaurants may offer halal-certified versions. Egg allergen is conditional on whether a fried egg is included. GI category is low (GI = 54, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). GL per serving calculated as (54 × 83.25) / 100 = 44.9 g.
